speaker: interviewer
text: --Cohn. I wanted to ask you also what impact in your life your experiences at Dachau have made-- what effect they have had on you that's long lasting?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, [SIGHS] it showed me that there's an inhumanity in men that I never thought that so-called civilized, educated people could commit these sort of atrocities against their fellow men, and often in cases of people they grew up with and they knew well. It just boggled my mind. I could never understand this. But it showed that it can be done by, by people you least expect that would resort to these sort of tactics. That's really--

speaker: interviewer
text: When you returned here, now you did write letters to your wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: I got two boxes that you wouldn't believe, and she saved every letter-- every letter. That's when you called about the interview and mentioned if I had some paraphernalia, I went down into my locker room in the basement. I got a lot of these things out.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you returned here in December of 1945, did you tell other people? Did you tell your father or your step-mother about what you had seen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I had written to them early on about what I had seen, absolutely.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was their reaction?

speaker: interviewee
text: I tell you, there was a time right after the war, I noticed, when people even like myself who didn't talk much about these things for a period of time. There's much more discussion about the Holocaust period now or has been the last several years than immediately after the war. I don't know why that is, but maybe we just wanted to shut it out of our mind. Maybe it was so incomprehensible. But for a period of time, I rarely talked about it to my family, rarely. And then as time went on, I talked to all of them, especially my children and grandchildren. But initially, no, I did not. And I found out a lot of other people had the same experience.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you encounter any survivors here, Eastern European survivors?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yeah. Many, many, many of them, sure. We have also a service at Israel Bonim which commemorates the 9th of November called Kristallnacht. And there we make special effort to recognize people who are members of the community and the synagogue who were in the camps, who all have the numbers tattooed on their arms. So yes, quite a number, number of friends, people we know, associate with who had been the camps, absolutely.

speaker: interviewer
text: Anyone from to Dachau?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Not that I know of. Most of them were in the eastern camps, that I can think of right now.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you spoken with them about their experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, people rarely talk about it. People who have been in camps, you almost have to pry out of them. We were very close to a young woman once who must have gone through hell. Her name was Eva Metzker. She's no longer alive. And she had the most traumatic experiences in camp. She must've been a very beautiful young woman. And she really didn't want to talk about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now that, especially right after the war--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --that was--

speaker: interviewee
text: Didn't want to talk about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: --very common, that they didn't

speaker: interviewee
text: Didn't want talk about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And many today do not. You mentioned that you did go back to Germany, and this was in the 1960s.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, that's correct. I think we've been there twice, actually, after the war-- '60s and then the '80s also.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe a little bit about those visits?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well I go there primarily with the intent now of once more visiting-- maybe I'll do it one more time to visit my mother's grave site and all the other family. I felt very comfortable with the younger generation, people our children's age. They were extremely nice and extremely outgoing, whereas people my age, I didn't-- I couldn't relate to. I didn't want to relate to because in back of my mind I always said to myself, I wonder what you did during the war. When I visited Essen some years ago, they turned the synagogue, which was completely burned out, into a museum. And the curator of the museum invited us one evening to have dinner with him. Well, it turned out that dinner was with a Gentile Christian, Gentile family, young people our children's age, who have a standing invitation with the now German Jewish community of Essen that any survivors or any visitor that comes through Essen, they would like to have them as guests in their house. This was a young couple. They were both teachers. We didn't know the people. They had a wonderful Shabbat meal on a Friday night for us. And we were astonished because they didn't know us, but it was a most pleasant evening. In the end, I think they're doing it like sort of an atonement for what their parents did to us. That's really how they feel. I mean it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I had no problem with the younger Germans, not at all. I didn't have it then, I don't think I would have it now. But the older generation, I-- they were all Nazis. I don't care. They were all Nazis. No matter what they tell you. All tell you, oh, I didn't-- I didn't know. I didn't belong. All a bunch of lies. I can't trust-- I wouldn't trust any one of them because I saw with my own eyes how their generation supported Hitler and his regime. And your wife feels the same way?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. She was too young to have the same experience I have. I think her bitterness is of a different nature. Also because in the small town that she lived in-- which she left, I told you, at the age of eight or nine. She was a little girl, so to speak. And all the people that she went to school with, her neighbors, they were mostly very nice neighbors. And some of them she visited again when she went back there, and they cried and surrounded her and they-- most of them were very lovely, very nice people that we met. And she said that most of these people where friends of her parents and seemed to remain that way, contrary to the experiences that I had. But she was a young girl. Maybe if she would have stayed another two or three years in Germany like I did, she would have had the same experience and feelings that I have.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any desire to visit Germany again?

speaker: interviewee
text: As I said, the only desire I would have would strictly be to maybe visit the grave site of my, my family one more time. Outside of that, no I really don't. I just--

speaker: interviewer
text: All in all, apart-- in addition to your grandmother, how many members of your family were murdered in the Holocaust?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the very-- the very closest of my relatives-- remember I told the story of my uncle who had the furniture store? His entire family perished. Yes, they all-- that's my Uncle Alfred who was a brother of my dad, my Aunt Irma, who was his wife, my cousin Herbert, and my cousin Gerta. They all lived in the town of Bottrop, and they all were sent to the camps. And they were all killed. None of them survived, none of them. And of course, that's with my grandmother, who also did not survive. Those were the closest members of my family.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you have also friends and acquaintances from either Essen or Gelsenkirchen who perished?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, sure. To this very day my very closest friends, we kept up all through the war years. He was a survivor. His family got killed. He's the only survivor. And we have close ties with him. He lives in San Francisco. And yeah, I know a number of people.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you also know people who, who perished in the Holocaust from--

speaker: interviewee
text: From our hometown?

speaker: interviewer
text: --your hometown?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Oh sure. Absolutely. Any number of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: We really-- your brother, Walter, he was also in the Armed Forces here.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, he was in the Air Corps.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he flew--

speaker: interviewee
text: He flew 32 missions, I believe, in the-- with the eighth Air Force. And he was a Bombardier. And he had won all sorts of medals, awards, [INAUDIBLE] for heroism during the war years. And unfortunately he passed away about 44 years ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: And his attitude towards Germany and the Germans is the same-- was the same as yours?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would think similar, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: There are-- you've heard of-- you've mentioned that there is still anti-Semitism today, and there are still those who-- there are those who deny the Holocaust.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that's really-- you know, when I first received this call from a young woman-- I think she was part of the Schindler--

speaker: interviewer
text: The Spielberg--

speaker: interviewee
text: The Spielberg, I'm sorry, organization. It occurred to me, what I'm doing I really didn't want to do it at first. I don't-- I wasn't publicity minded or what have you. And I thought it would be good to speak for their record, so to speak.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because of, to try to counter this Holocaust denial?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, absolutely. Because what I told you and what I will show you in pictures in a few minutes, it will put all of these people who are Holocaust deniers, it'll put them to shame. I mean, how can these people say it didn't happen when people like myself and thousands of others, either were there as prisoners or in my case as a liberator, so to speak? How can they deny these things?

speaker: interviewer
text: How can they tell us who lived through it, who was, like myself, beaten to a pulp by the Gestapo, how can they say it didn't happen? I mean, and having my grandmother and uncles and aunts and cousins deported and killed. God almighty, what's the matter with these people? I mean, they should all be put up against the wall, I think. It's terrible. That's why I really wanted to do what I'm doing now, actually. Well, I really thank you--

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome.

speaker: interviewer
text: --for doing what you have done.

speaker: interviewee
text: I hope it has-- it's meaningful to those who will watch it when I'm long gone.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm sure it will be. And we're now going to go to the pictures.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this goes way back, and it's a picture of my dad in the German army uniform, World War I. My dad is the one in the center. His brother Hartwig is on the left hand side of the picture. And his brother, Alfred, about whom I talked a little while ago who was deported and killed by the Nazis, he's on the right hand side. This picture was taken during World War I, and I received it after my father's death among his effects.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the other uncle did survive?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. He died-- they're both dead, of course. My uncle Hartwig and my dad have both passed away. And Alfred is the one who got killed, yeah. This is a picture of my mother and myself. It was probably taken in 1921. I was several months old. And even so, I'm wearing a dress which is what the German parents often did. They dressed the babies with dresses, whether they were boys or girls. But I am indeed a boy, and if you don't believe me, ask my wife. [LAUGHTER] This is an excellent picture of myself on the left hand side and my brother Walter on the right hand side. I believe it was taken about 1927. I was about six years of age and Wally about four years of age. And we are dressed in the typical navy type of clothing that children our age had to wear in those years.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was in Essen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, this was taken in Essen, Katernberg, where we lived the first years of my life. Again, a picture of myself, also at the age of six-- my first day in school in a velvet suit in Essen, Katernberg. My wife says I was a good looking kid, and here I am at about six, seven. And I had lots of hair in those days. I don't know what happened to it. It's gone now. I had big ears, also. They're not so big anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was still in Essen?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Essen at the same place, yes. This is the interior of our synagogue in Essen, which was gutted by fire and vandalized shortly after the 9th-- or on the 9th of November. It shows the condition of the interior. I don't have a picture of the synagogue in Gelsenkirchen because it was completely burned to the ground. There was nothing left.

speaker: interviewer
text: But this was--

speaker: interviewee
text: This is in Essen.

speaker: interviewer
text: --absolutely gutted from the inside.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yeah. It's a museum today. It's fixed up beautifully. The outside is just like it always looked. Very in the style of the Byzantine, in the Byzantine style-- beautiful synagogue. But the inside is a museum now.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was taken in 1938?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. In 1938. After 9th of November, 1938. This is the original passport of my dad's Siegfried, which he received at the time of immigration from Germany to the United States. You can see it next to his name Siegfried is the name Israel which is something every German Jewish male had to adopt. And every German Jewish woman had to-- the second name, the middle initial, middle name had to be Sarah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my dad, as well as myself, it became Israel. And this is the original passport, as I told you. You can see the swastika imprint on this picture then also below the picture. And this was used by him in 1939--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes at the time--

speaker: interviewer
text: --to immigrate to Holland?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's correct. When he came-- when he came to meet me at the dock in Holland, and we left for the United States together. This is one of the, I believe, first pictures that was taken of me when I-- after my arrival in the United States at the age of 20, and that would have been in 1941. Shows me all dressed up.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was in Chicago?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Chicago, Illinois. Yes. In 1941, I got lucky. I met my wife Elsa. This is a picture of her's at the age of 17. Here the two of us on the 57th Street Beach in Chicago, and it was probably one of our first dates in 1940 or '41. I believe it was '41, I'm not sure. We had a great time together.

speaker: interviewer
text: About a year after I had-- after I entered the service, I came home and became engaged to Elsa. I think it was May 14th, March 14th? May 14th. May 14th, 1943.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture taken of my brother Walter and myself. You notice Walter on the right hand side with the Army Air Corps patch on the upper sleeve and me with the tank destroyer emblem on my sleeve. The picture was taken in 1943. Take a good look. This is why we won the war. You can see me as a real soldier in basic training in Fort Sill, Oklahoma, before I was being shipped to Fort Hood-- Camp Hood, Texas in those days.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was 1942?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in 1942. This was in July, August of 1942. After our arrival overseas, I went with the 54th Field Hospital over to France, and here we treated both American wounded as well as German prisoners of war. And this is a shot of some German prisoners sitting outside of our tent area. It's another shot of the wounded being brought into the 54th Field Hospital before we treated the same. Some were Americans, but these were more than likely prisoners of war.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they were treated very well by--

speaker: interviewee
text: They were treated equally well as far as the surgical or medically as our own people. We treated them in a very humane manner, very humane. I had talked earlier about the homes we requisitioned for our troops in Germany. This is one of them. This was taken in [GERMAN], and the house had also been vandalized by slave laborers who had been employed by the previous owner. And they absolutely raised hell and were very pleased to see us and take over the town.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was the spring of 1945?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I have read to all of you the letter I had written to my wife about my experiences at the Dachau concentration camp. These series of pictures that you will see now-- about 10 or 12 of them-- will show my entry into the camp. The first thing that we saw were these freight cars with our troops patrolling alongside and many, many dead people on the inside. Here again you see all the prisoners that had been shipped from other concentration camps-- which were in the path of either the Russians or the American troops-- shipped to Dachau and the people simply died in these freight cars. You see a shot of all the dead people inside the car. And these were-- these were left for several days in order to show this atrocity, and so-- No, they were-- this was within about 48 hours of liberation of the camp, and we certainly didn't have time yet to remove the bodies. It happened somewhat later on. As I look closer in the freight car, I noticed how the people, the dead people, were piled up. I even thought the fellow in the back on the left hand side might still be alive. And there was a bucket in the middle. You'll see it on the picture. This was their toilet facility for all the people in that particular car. Here's a closeup shot of the same car. These were guards, which did not escape our troops and were shot and were left laying on the outside of the fences of the camp. You see our soldiers walking in the back. Later on these bodies were removed. There were either German soldiers or German civilian guards.

speaker: interviewer
text: And of course none of this can compare to the normal kind of casualties that you saw in the field of battle? This was entirely different.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a different situation, absolutely. This was the exterior of the prisoner's compound. There was an electric wire fence and a water trench. We walked around it, I walked in front of it and I talked to these prisoners who wanted to be released, but we couldn't take them out because of the illness and typhoid and what have you inside the camp. There's our jeep on the right hand side. My chaplain-- our chaplain and myself, we had taken cameras along. And this was a shot of the house, which had the so-called shower inside, but it was, of course, not the shower. These were the actual entrance to the gas chambers, this building. I didn't take any picture of the ovens themselves because I think it was very dark inside, but then on the outside of the building, in the rear of the building, they had several chambers. They were piled high with the remains of human bodies. And this is what we saw, what you see in this picture. It was a horrible, horrible site-- hundreds of bodies piled like cockroach stacked on top of each other. They were to be burned by the Nazis. That's why they were there, but they just didn't have enough time. And someone there, one of the guards told us they had run out of coal. That's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then one more. Do you--

speaker: interviewee
text: This is another close up of the bodies in back of the gas chambers. And if there's any denier of the Holocaust in this world, all you have to do-- all anyone would have to do is show them this one picture. And this will put them to shame. This is what I saw and photographed with my own eyes at the Dachau concentration camp in early May of 1945. Finally over and I'm back home. All these memories that you've seen are now of my past, and a new life begins. These pictures in 1950 show our the boys that were born first, Howard and David, and my wife, of course. And it wasn't long thereafter when the third one was born. Here's number three, our daughter Joyce, who came after Howard and David. I believe this picture was taken after her graduation from kindergarten. And of course, our life at that time started anew with our stores in the early '50s. And now we will see the latest picture with my entire family. All of our children married. And now you will see a picture taken about four years ago, the occasion was our granddaughter's, Lauren's bat mitzvah. And I will just simply identify all the members of our family from left to right. Our granddaughter Amy, her father, our son-in-law David Feuer next to his wife, our daughter Joyce. In front of them is also their son Aaron, our youngest grandson. Behind Aaron is myself, Herman, and our granddaughter Lauren. Next to Lauren, my wife Elsa. Next to Elsa, our oldest son Howard. In front of Howard is the other daughter, Dana. Behind Dana is our daughter-in-law Phyllis. And next to Phyllis is our son David and his wife Valerie. And in front of the two of them, are our two grandsons who now live in Denver, Joshua and Zachary. And this is our family and we are really very, very happy that we are able to show this to whoever wants to look at it many years from now.
